requirements.name: Zone definition and documentation
requirements.description: Define and document all security zones within the OT environment. Each zone must have: a defined boundary, a named set of assets, an assigned SL-T, and a stated rationale for the zone grouping.

legal.summary: Zone definition is a prerequisite for the system-level security risk assessment required by IEC 62443-3-2. NACSA s21(1) mandates risk assessment — zone-based risk assessment is the IEC 62443-compliant method.

technical.summary: Create and maintain a network topology diagram showing all zones, zone boundaries, conduits, and inter-zone data flows.
actions: Document all zones with asset lists
Assign SL-T to each zone based on consequence analysis
Map all inter-zone communications to conduits
Review and update zone model after any architectural change

governance.summary: Zone model must be reviewed by OT security team, approved by OT manager, and included in the NACSA s21 risk assessment package.
actions: Annual review and revalidation of zone model
Update triggered by any architecture change
Include in NACSA s23 audit artefact package

evidenceItems: Network topology diagram with zones and conduits marked

slMapping.sl1: Zones defined and documented — even if enforcement is limited
slMapping.sl2: Zones technically enforced with firewall/VLAN at every boundary
slMapping.sl3: Zones enforced with OT-aware firewall with DPI and IDS at boundaries
slMapping.sl4: Hardware-enforced boundaries for highest-criticality zones (data diodes, air gaps)

requirements.name: Industrial DMZ implementation
requirements.description: Implement an Industrial DMZ (IDMZ) between OT Level 3 and IT Level 4. All data exchange between OT and IT must pass through IDMZ services. No direct IT-to-OT Layer 3 routing permitted.

legal.summary: The IDMZ is the primary architectural control implementing SR-5.1 (network segmentation) and NACSA s18 security measures for entities with both IT and OT environments.
legal.owner: OT Security Architect / IT Security Team

technical.summary: Deploy two-firewall IDMZ design: OT-side firewall (deny all, permit specific OT protocols outbound only) and IT-side firewall (deny all, permit specific IT service access to IDMZ services only).
actions: Deploy two separate firewalls (OT-side and IT-side)
Define all IDMZ services (historian mirror, patch server, jump server, remote access gateway)
Implement deny-all with explicit permit at both firewalls
Deploy data diode for highest-criticality OT-to-IT data flows (historian replication)
Document all permitted traffic flows with business justification

governance.summary: IDMZ design approved by OT and IT security leadership. Traffic flows reviewed quarterly. Changes require change management approval.
actions: Quarterly firewall rule review
Annual IDMZ architecture review
All IDMZ changes through formal change management

evidenceItems: Traffic flow register with business justifications

slMapping.sl1: Some separation — at minimum, OT on separate VLAN from corporate IT
slMapping.sl2: Full IDMZ with firewall on both sides; all IT-OT traffic through IDMZ services
slMapping.sl3: IDMZ with OT-aware firewalls and DPI; data diode for historian replication
slMapping.sl4: Data diode mandatory for all OT-to-IT flows; no inbound connectivity from IT to OT

requirements.name: OT protocol-aware firewall filtering
requirements.description: Firewalls at OT zone boundaries must perform deep packet inspection (DPI) of OT protocols — not just TCP/UDP port filtering. Permit only valid, expected OT protocol messages. Block malformed or unexpected protocol commands.

legal.summary: Standard port-based firewalls are insufficient for OT — a valid Modbus TCP connection on port 502 can contain any function code, including malicious write commands. OT-aware DPI validates protocol content, not just connection establishment.

technical.summary: Deploy OT-aware firewalls (Fortinet FortiGate with OT profile, Tofino Xenon, Cisco Firepower with OT signatures, Palo Alto with OT app-IDs) at all inter-zone conduits.
actions: Identify all OT protocols in use per zone (Modbus, DNP3, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, IEC 104)
Configure DPI rules to permit only expected function codes and message types
Block unexpected protocol function codes (e.g., block Modbus Write if only reads are expected)
Enable logging of all policy violations

governance.summary: DPI rulesets reviewed when protocol usage changes. Vendor-assisted rule development for site-specific OT protocol profiles.
actions: Document protocol usage per conduit
Review DPI rules after any process or control system change

slMapping.sl1: Standard stateful firewall (no OT protocol awareness)
slMapping.sl2: OT-aware DPI for major protocols (Modbus, EtherNet/IP)
slMapping.sl3: OT-aware DPI for all protocols in use; IDS integration; permit-specific-function-codes only
slMapping.sl4: Hardware-enforced unidirectional flow for highest-risk conduits; DPI on all bidirectional conduits

requirements.name: Wireless network segmentation
requirements.description: Wireless OT networks (WiFi, WirelessHART, ISA100, private LTE for OT) must be logically isolated from corporate WiFi and subject to equivalent zone security controls.

legal.summary: Wireless OT networks extend the zone boundary into the radio frequency domain. Unauthorised WiFi access points in OT areas bypass physical zone boundaries.

technical.summary: Deploy separate SSIDs for OT WiFi with WPA3 Enterprise authentication. Wireless sensors (WirelessHART, ISA100) on dedicated VLAN not connected to general OT network. Survey for rogue access points regularly.
actions: Separate OT WiFi SSID from corporate WiFi — separate VLAN and separate controller where possible
WPA3 Enterprise (RADIUS authentication) for OT WiFi
WirelessHART networks on dedicated VLAN — do not route to other OT VLANs without firewall
Monthly wireless survey for rogue access points in OT areas

governance.summary: No personal devices on OT WiFi. All OT wireless devices in asset register.
actions: Wireless device inventory maintained
Periodic spectrum surveys documented

slMapping.sl1: OT WiFi separate from corporate WiFi
slMapping.sl2: WPA3 authentication; separate VLAN; no internet access
slMapping.sl3: RADIUS auth; wireless IDS; automatic rogue AP detection
slMapping.sl4: Continuous wireless spectrum monitoring; no wireless on safety systems
